prior.title: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP) — Intelligence Brief
prior.summary: Caltrans sustainable freight planning division page is currently unavailable during site accessibility upgrades. The specific content regarding sustainable freight planning initiatives cannot be retrieved at this time.
prior.full_brief:
# California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP) — Intelligence Brief

## Market Signal Brief

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

The California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP) is a multi-agency state policy framework, initiated by Executive Order B-32-15, signed by Governor Brown in July 2015, directing the secretaries of the California State Transportation Agency (CALSTA), California Environmental Protection Agency (CalEPA), and Natural Resources Agency to lead other relevant state departments in developing an integrated action plan by July 2016. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap

The Executive Order set three stated objectives: "establishes clear targets to improve freight efficiency, transition to zero-emission technologies, and increase competitiveness of California's freight system." *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap

The CSFAP is not a voluntary initiative. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether specific CSFAP actions and associated legislation (listed in Section 2 below) impose enforceable obligations on freight operators in California, as distinct from directing state agencies, requires counsel review. The plan does operationalize binding California statutes including AB 32 (2006) and a series of subsequent legislative mandates referenced in the Caltrans CSFAP page.

The primary Caltrans Sustainable Freight Planning URL was unavailable at time of generation (Content Not Available error returned). The CSFAP program page at https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap remained accessible and provides the substantive content synthesized in this brief. The Caltrans Strategic Planning sub-page at https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/sustainable-freight-planning/strategic-planning also returned Content Not Available. The CARB CSFAP page returned a 403 access error. This brief draws on the accessible CSFAP program page and corroborating academic/research sources.

*Analytical inference:* The Caltrans site-wide digital accessibility upgrade currently in progress (noted in the Content Not Available pages) is the most likely proximate cause of the missing sub-pages; the program itself remains active as evidenced by the CSFAP page and ongoing funded research activity.

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

**Lead agencies:** California State Transportation Agency (CALSTA), California Environmental Protection Agency (CalEPA), and the Natural Resources Agency, as directed by Executive Order B-32-15. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap

**Participating state departments:** California Air Resources Board (ARB), California Department of Transportation (Caltrans), California Energy Commission, and the Governor's Office of Business and Economic Development (GO-Biz). *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap

**Stakeholder engagement structure:** The interagency team engaged stakeholders throughout the State through webinars, presentations to staff and boards from regional agencies, the California Freight Advisory Committee (CFAC), and briefings with interested parties such as industry leaders, environmental groups, and other community groups. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap

**Research consortium participants:** METRANS Transportation Center (USC/CSULB) holds a Caltrans-funded contract to track economic competitiveness metrics under Action 6 of the CSFAP. The project was funded at $299,500, contract number 65A0674 TO 008, performance period 5/17/2019 to 5/14/2021. GO-Biz convened a working group consisting of freight industry representatives and state agency staff to guide the work of the consultant. *Source: Implementation of Action 6 of the California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP) Phase 3: Tracking Economic Competitiveness, METRANS/PSR, 2021. https://www.metrans.org/research/implementation-of-action-6-of-the-california-sustainable-freight-action-plan-csfap-phase-3-tracking-economic-competitiveness

**Workforce implementation:** California State University Long Beach (CSULB), through the NCST (UC Davis), held a Caltrans contract to design and implement two four-day in-person Sustainable Freight Academies during calendar year 2021, funded at $111,154. *Source: Caltrans Sustainable Freight Academy, NCST/UC Davis, 2022. https://ncst.ucdavis.edu/project/caltrans-sustainable-freight-academy

A 2024 follow-on Sustainable Freight Academy is listed as a related project on the NCST site, indicating the program is continuing beyond its 2021–2022 initial phase. *Source: Caltrans Sustainable Freight Academy, NCST/UC Davis, 2022. https://ncst.ucdavis.edu/project/caltrans-sustainable-freight-academy

*Analytical inference:* The combination of multi-agency leadership, industry working groups, academic research partners, and a recurring staff training program reflects a multi-cycle institutionalization of CSFAP, not a single-release plan. Workspaces in freight forwarding and drayage roles that interact with California trade corridors are operating inside an active, multi-layered state policy system, not a static 2016 document.

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

**Enabling legislation structure:** The CSFAP is underpinned by a layered stack of California legislation. Key statutes listed on the Caltrans CSFAP page with direct freight implications include:

- SB 671 (2021): Transportation: Clean Freight Corridor Efficiency Assessment — establishes the Clean Freight Corridor efficiency assessment, to be developed by the California Transportation Commission, in coordination with other state agencies, to identify statewide freight corridor segments and prioritizes them for the deployment of zero-emission medium and heavy-duty vehicles. The Energy Commission is to prepare and present a report on the assessment findings and recommendations to the Legislature by December 1, 2023. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap

- SB 726 (2021): Alternative Fuel and Vehicle Technologies: Transportation Sustainability Strategy — the bill would require the state board and the State Energy Resources Conservation and Development Commission, in coordination with specified public agencies, including local air pollution control districts and air quality management districts, if those districts choose to participate, to jointly develop a comprehensive transportation sustainability strategy. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap

- SB 1403 (2018): requires the California Air Resources Board and State Energy Resources Conservation and Development Commission to establish a 3-year investment strategy for zero- and near-zero heavy duty vehicles and equipment commensurate with meeting certain goals and milestones, including placing in service at least 1,000,000 zero-emission and near-zero emission vehicles by January 1, 2023. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap

- Executive Order N-79-20 (2020): sets the course to end the sales of internal combustion vehicles by 2035, and establishes a target for the transportation sector to achieve 100% carbon neutrality by 2045. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap

**CFMP 2025 preparation:** The 2021 Sustainable Freight Academy curriculum included preparing staff for the 2025 CFMP (California Freight Mobility Plan), with an emphasis on the role played by freight planning within system planning, including traffic management planning, identification of alternative routes, pavement design innovation, modal shift opportunities, and system resiliency. *Source: Caltrans Sustainable Freight Academy, NCST/UC Davis, 2022. https://ncst.ucdavis.edu/project/caltrans-sustainable-freight-academy

*Analytical inference:* The 2025 CFMP update is an active near-term policy milestone. Workspaces moving freight on California corridors — including drayage at Los Angeles/Long Beach, road haulage, and air cargo ground operations — face policy-environment changes tied to this update. The exact content and timelines of the 2025 CFMP are not yet available from the sources at hand; this is flagged as a monitoring priority (see Section 7).

**Incentive program availability — current confirmed programs:**

- Hybrid and Zero Emission Truck and Bus Voucher Incentive Project (HVIP): a voucher program provides funding to support the long-term transition to ZEVs. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap
- Volkswagen Environmental Mitigation Trust for California: $90 Million available for the ZE Class 8 Freight and Port Drayage Trucks Program, $70 Million for ZE Freight and Marine Projects, $60 Million for Combustion Freight and Marine Projects, and $10 Million for Light Duty ZEV Infrastructure Refueling Stations. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap Note: current availability of these specific tranches requires verification with CARB; the CARB program page was inaccessible (403 error) at time of generation.
- FARMER program: allocated $56 Million to reduce GHG emissions in the Ag sector in Rural Disadvantaged and Low-Income Communities. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap
- Sustainable Transportation Equity Projects (STEP): a Cap and Trade Proceed Funded Program Allocation of $19.5 Million; $1.75M for Planning and Capacity Improvements, and $17.75M for Implementation Grants. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap
- Zero-Emission Drayage Truck and Infrastructure Pilot Project: a CARB and Energy Commission (CEC) Clean Transportation Incentive Program for FY 2019–2020. Applications closed. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap
- AB 118 (2019): the California Energy Commission to provide $100 million annually for the adoption of alternative and renewable fuels and advanced transportation technologies. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap

*Specific current disbursement status and open application windows for HVIP and VW Trust tranches not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-27.*

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes — MONITORING | COST ALERT

**For road-mode operations on California corridors (drayage, cartage, ground transport):**

The CSFAP's core ZEV transition mandate, combined with Executive Order N-79-20's 2035 internal combustion sales end date, creates a fleet replacement timeline that is operative now for workspaces procuring Class 8 drayage capacity at Los Angeles/Long Beach and other California port gateways. *Analytical inference:* Third-party drayage carriers servicing the workspace's California inbound flows are subject to CARB's Advanced Clean Trucks regulation (separate from the CSFAP but listed as a linked regulatory instrument); ZEV surcharges or equipment availability constraints on California drayage lanes are a plausible medium-term cost signal. Effect on specific lanes requires carrier-specific data.

**For air-mode operations at California airports:**

The Zero Emission Airport Shuttle Bus Program — a CARB funded incentive program to promote the development and use of Zero-Emission Airport Ground Equipment and Shuttle Bus Transportation — signals that California airport ground operations are within scope of CSFAP-linked emissions policy. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap *Analytical inference:* Workspaces operating air-freight ground handling chains through LAX, SFO, or other California airports face a regulatory environment in which ground support equipment electrification is actively incentivized and may become mandated through linked CARB rulemakings. Effect on specific airport handling costs requires ground handler-specific data.

**For ocean-mode gateway operations (Port of LA, Port of Long Beach):**

The VW Trust ZE Class 8 Freight and Port Drayage Trucks Program ($90M) and ZE Freight and Marine Projects ($70M) are specifically directed at port gateway corridors. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap *Analytical inference:* Port drayage capacity on California import/export gateways will face a compressing window of non-ZEV availability; workspaces dependent on spot drayage capacity should monitor carrier fleet transition timelines.

**Economic competitiveness tracking (CSFAP Action 6):**

The METRANS Phase 3 research established that the working group convened by GO-Biz was developing metrics for measuring economic competitiveness and identifying overall targets for economic competitiveness. *Source: Implementation of Action 6 of the California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP) Phase 3: Tracking Economic Competitiveness, METRANS/PSR, 2021. https://www.metrans.org/research/implementation-of-action-6-of-the-california-sustainable-freight-action-plan-csfap-phase-3-tracking-economic-competitiveness *Analytical inference:* These metrics, once institutionalized, could create a state-level freight cost reporting environment that increases transparency on the cost burden of ZEV transition — useful for workspace-level margin modelling on California-origin and California-destination freight.

*Specific cost figures for ZEV drayage premiums vs. diesel drayage on California corridors not available from primary sources as of 2026-05-27.*

---

# 5. Competitive Implications — MONITORING | COMPETITIVE EDGE

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces that establish early visibility into HVIP voucher availability and Clean Freight Corridor priority corridors (as designated under SB 671) are positioned to advise clients on routing and equipment choices ahead of competitors that treat the CSFAP as background policy. The Clean Freight Corridor assessment targets "zero-emission medium and heavy-duty vehicles" deployment on California's primary trade corridors — workspaces advising importers and exporters through the Port of Los Angeles/Long Beach gateway will encounter these corridor designations in procurement discussions.

*Analytical inference:* California's role as a national regulatory vanguard means that CSFAP-linked requirements — particularly CARB's Advanced Clean Trucks and Advanced Clean Fleets rules — will shape the available drayage carrier pool before similar requirements materialize in other U.S. states. Competitors who have mapped their California carrier panel against ZEV transition timelines hold a differentiated position in client conversations about supply chain resilience.

No named competitor-specific sourced reporting on CSFAP positioning was available in the source blocks provided. This is noted as a monitoring gap.

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- California operates the most comprehensive state-level sustainable freight policy framework in the United States, grounded in Executive Order B-32-15 (2015) and operationalized through a multi-agency action plan covering ZEV transition, freight efficiency, and economic competitiveness targets. This is a sourced fact.
- The state has committed $90M in VW Trust funds specifically to ZE Class 8 freight and port drayage trucks, and multiple additional funding streams are live or recently closed. Clients shipping through California gateways are operating in an environment where their carrier panel will face fleet transition pressure on a defined timeline.
- The 2025 California Freight Mobility Plan (CFMP) update is in preparation and will likely reset corridor-level priorities; the workspace is monitoring this actively.

**What to avoid claiming:**

- Do not represent specific incentive program application windows as open without verifying with CARB directly — the CARB program page was inaccessible at time of generation and program statuses change. The Zero-Emission Drayage Truck and Infrastructure Pilot Project for FY 2019–2020 is confirmed closed.
- Do not represent the CSFAP as imposing direct legal obligations on shippers or freight forwarders without counsel review. *Legal Confirmation Required:* the CSFAP's direct legal effect on freight forwarders and shippers (as distinct from carriers and state agencies) has not been established from the sources available.
- Do not cite the 2016 CSFAP PDF as a current document without noting that the 2025 CFMP update is in preparation and may revise targets and strategies.

**Questions to pose to clients:**

- What share of your California-destined or California-origin freight moves by Class 8 drayage, and has your drayage carrier reported a ZEV fleet transition plan?
- Are you tracking HVIP voucher eligibility for any owned or managed fleet assets operating in California?
- Do any of your California facility leases or port terminal agreements reference Clean Air Action Plan (CAAP) or CSFAP compliance requirements?

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**MONITORING — 2025 CFMP publication window**

The 2021 Sustainable Freight Academy curriculum was explicitly designed to prepare staff for the 2025 CFMP, with an emphasis on freight planning within system planning. *Source: Caltrans Sustainable Freight Academy, NCST/UC Davis, 2022. https://ncst.ucdavis.edu/project/caltrans-sustainable-freight-academy The 2025 CFMP, when published, will set the next policy cycle's corridor priorities and ZEV deployment targets. *Analytical inference:* Re-check this item in Q3 2025 or when the Caltrans freight planning sub-pages (currently returning Content Not Available) are restored — both are leading indicators that the CFMP update is near release.

**Immediate actions — research gaps to close:**

1. Verify current HVIP voucher availability directly with CARB or the Calstart HVIP administrator. The CSFAP page lists HVIP as active; current voucher pool size and application status require direct confirmation.
2. Confirm VW Trust ZE Class 8 tranche disbursement status. The $90M allocation is listed on the CSFAP page; remaining availability and application timelines are not confirmed from available sources.
3. Map the workspace's California drayage carrier panel against CARB's Advanced Clean Fleets rule compliance timelines. This is the most direct operational consequence of the CSFAP-linked regulatory stack for workspaces not operating their own trucks.
4. Monitor Caltrans freight planning sub-page restoration (https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/sustainable-freight-planning/strategic-planning) for CFMP 2025 release.
5. Monitor SB 671 Clean Freight Corridor assessment report — the Energy Commission was directed to report findings to the Legislature by December 1, 2023. *Source: California Sustainable Freight Action Plan (CSFAP), Caltrans, 2024. https://dot.ca.gov/programs/transportation-planning/division-of-transportation-planning/strategic-freight-planning/csfap Whether this report has been published and its corridor prioritization findings are a research gap to close.

**Re-check window for this brief:** Q3 2025 or upon Caltrans sub-page restoration, whichever comes first. The 2025 CFMP publication is the primary conversion trigger.

---
